It's easier to go find somebody else to give me money. So I know I'm not going to satisfy him. To take him and have him unsatisfied, life's too short and reputation's too precious. So figure out what for you is not a good client and then don't take him. Don't overpromise and under deliver. The temptation is enormous to tell the client what they want to hear about timelines, deadlines, results, in order to get the money, especially when you need it. You will regret it. Don't prescribe based on ability to pay. An awful lot of consultants do this. The temptation is understandable. And in 8 out of 10, 7 out of 10 cases, you probably get away with it. But eventually, you're going to be caught and it's going to not be pretty. So I have a formula for calculating a project. It's a real formula. And it doesn't matter to me whether it's a Fortune 500 company or it's a little guy just starting this company. Fees the same. Deals the same. And if anybody ever compares notes, they're going to find the only variable is the anticipated aggravation surcharge. And I disclose it. So if I add it, I say to the person face to face, there's $5,000 added to this contract because I think you're going to be a pain in the ass. If by some chance you're not a pain in the ass, I may give it back to you when we're all done. So if anybody ever compares notes, it's right there. Don't cut deals. You will be tempted to do it. I'm going to let one person in my coaching group for free because it's so good to have them in there. I'm going to let them in at half price because nobody keeps a secret. Ever. Somebody will compare notes. Don't cut deals. Plus, you don't just lose the money. You lose the power. You lose the control. They either pay or they don't pay. We talked about this earlier. Be wary of recommending others. Be very careful. Be very cautious. Be very demanding. Somebody asked about our criteria. I didn't really want to have a big discussion about it. One is, of course, that people guarantee what they sell to you. We don't let anybody sell to you without a guarantee. That's really a small criteria, but it's still an important criteria. Don't steal from your clients if you're going to use work product in other ways. Disclose it. Never sign a non-disclosure agreement. Never. Refuse under all circumstances. But on the other hand, if they have truly proprietary information, which a lot of people think they have, but they don't, but occasionally somebody actually does, it's always a great shock to me. But occasionally, somebody actually has something unique or that they really are getting to the market with first. Don't disclose it. In new group settings, have clear rules and frequent reminder discussions of what is fair play with SWD. What's fair play about copying, using, and the best rule connected to all other rules is, if in doubt, ask the other person. I have a client right now. I have one client who paid me $75,000 to write his campaign. He's letting another guy in the coaching group knock it off. Guy asked him. He's letting them knock it off, paid me $75,000 for it. That's fine. They made an arrangement. He didn't ask the guy for any money. If he had asked him for money, the guy would have paid him. He just chose not to. That's fine. But if the same guy had taken it all and used it and simply taken out chiropractic and put in martial arts and walked off without asking him, the two of them are in the same coaching group, he'd be maddering a wet hand. So you try and get yourself and everybody else to play as fair as you can. Number seven is the best tip of all. The word avoid is the important word. Try and avoid all misunderstandings. So number one, disclose other relationships. Don't let them think for a minute. They got some kind of exclusive. They don't. Never work without detailed written agreements. Never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never, never. LeGrand's a good example for me. Well, I've known Ron for 25 years, like Ron. Ron's as close to a friend as I can allow a client to be. However, I wouldn't do work for Ron without a written contract on a bet. Because of distrust, but because we're both old feeble, his memory's no good, mine's not either. There'll be a day sometime when one of us will go remember it exactly the same way the other one remembered it. Has to happen. Best way to avoid it? Paper. Paper trail everything.
